title: I have an error in this code that says answer is not defined:  ElseIf Not insured And Not cleared Then 'Insurance and DB…

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
I have an error in this code that says answer is not defined:  ElseIf Not insured And Not cleared Then 'Insurance and DBS are both not complete
answer = MsgBox(Format(INSURANCE_DBS_REQUEST_MSG, contractor), vbYesNo + vbQuestion, "Send details")
If answer = vbNo Then Exit Sub

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
As an AI language model, I cannot determine what programming language you are using as you did not mention it. However, based on the syntax, it appears to be Visual Basic (VB). 

To fix the error, you need to declare the answer variable before assigning it a value. Add the following line before the ElseIf statement:

Dim answer As VbMsgBoxResult

This will declare the answer variable as a VbMsgBoxResult type and the error should be resolved.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: what is the difference between Application.Calculate and Activesheet.Calculate and when should you use either

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Both `Application.Calculate` and `ActiveSheet.Calculate` are used to recalculate all formulas in a worksheet or workbook. They differ in the scope of calculation.

- `Application.Calculate`: It recalculates all formulas in all worksheets in the entire workbook, and it is a more comprehensive form of calculating as it includes all sheets in the workbook irrespective of whether they are visible or not. It is typically used when you want to recalculate the entire workbook, or you want to optimize calculation performance by turning off automatic calculation before recalculating the entire workbook.

- `ActiveSheet.Calculate`: It recalculates all formulas on the active worksheet only. It is typically used when working with a single worksheet and you want to force recalculation of the formulas on that worksheet.

In summary, `Application.Calculate` recalculates all formulas in the workbook, while `ActiveSheet.Calculate` only recalculates formulas on the current/active worksheet. Therefore, you should use the appropriate method depending on the scope of calculation that you want to achieve.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: What is the vba code for a simple message

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Here is the VBA code for a simple message box:

```
Sub Simple_MessageBox()
    MsgBox "Hello World!"
End Sub
```

Explanation:

- `Sub`: This is the start of the VBA subroutine, which is a block of code that performs a specific task.
- `MsgBox`: This is a built-in VBA function that displays a message box.
- `"Hello World!"`: This is the message that the message box will display. You can change this message to whatever you like.

To run the code, simply open the VBA Editor window in Excel (by pressing Alt+F11), paste the code above into a new module, and then execute the subroutine by pressing F5 or clicking the "Run" button on the toolbar. A message box will then pop up displaying the message "Hello World!".

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: is there a one line message routine that does not require a sub

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Yes, you can display a simple message box in VBA using a one-line message box function that does not require a subroutine. Here's an example:

```
MsgBox "Hello World!"
```

This code will display a message box with the message "Hello World!" when executed. The `MsgBox` function is used to display the message box, and the message itself is passed as an argument to the function. 

Note: Although this code can be executed directly in the VBA editor, it is not recommended to use this approach for larger projects. Subroutines are preferred as they help to organize code and make it easier to read and maintain over time.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
When an excel sheet is activated, I want to check for the last entry in column B.
On the same row of the last entry found in column B, I want to check if column D of the same row has the value 'Admin Request'. If found I want to open a word document doc1, that will have focus over the currently open workbook. If not found, I want to open a different word document doc2, that will have focus over the currently open workbook. Can you please provide me with a VBA routine to do this.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Sure, here's a VBA code that should do what you're looking for:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Dim lastRow As Long
    Dim adminCheck As String
    
    ' Find last row in Column B
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "B").End(xlUp).Row
    
    ' Check if cell in Column D on the same row as last entry in Column B is "Admin Request"
    adminCheck = Cells(lastRow, "D").Value
    If adminCheck = "Admin Request" Then
        ' Open doc1 with focus over the workbook
        Set objWord = CreateObject("Word.Application")
        objWord.Visible = True
        objWord.Activate
        objWord.Documents.Open ("C:\path_to_doc1")
    Else
        ' Open doc2 with focus over the workbook
        Set objWord = CreateObject("Word.Application")
        objWord.Visible = True
        objWord.Activate
        objWord.Documents.Open ("C:\path_to_doc2")
    End If
End Sub
```

Explanation:

- `Worksheet_Activate()`: This is a VBA event that is triggered when the worksheet is activated.
- `lastRow`: This variable is used to hold the last row in Column B.
- `adminCheck`: This variable is used to hold the value in Column D on the same row as the last entry in Column B.
- `Cells(Rows.Count, "B").End(xlUp).Row`: This finds the last non-empty row in Column B.
- `Cells(lastRow, "D").Value`: This retrieves the value in Column D on the same row as the last entry in Column B.
- `Set objWord = CreateObject("Word.Application")`: This creates a new instance of Word and assigns it to the variable `objWord`.
- `objWord.Visible = True`: This makes Word window visible.
- `objWord.Activate`: This brings the Word window to the front and gives it focus.
- `objWord.Documents.Open ("C:\path_to_file")`: This opens a Word document with the specified path.

Note: Make sure to replace the file paths of `doc1` and `doc2` with the actual paths of the Word documents that you want to open.
